schools.summary: David and Omri had subdued Moab; the Moabite (Mesha) Stone corroborates the revolt, mentioned here parenthetically and resumed in 2 Kings 3.
Gill: Moab, tributary since David, seized on Ahab's ill success against Syria and his death to throw off the yoke.
After the kingdom split Moab fell to Israel and Edom to Judah; both revolted when the crowns grew weak and forsaken by God.
Matthew Henry: as the king rebelled against God, his vassal rebelled against him; sin weakens and impoverishes us.
The lattice is the window of the roof-chamber; Baal-zebub is a fly-shaped deity of divination (myiomancy), the nearest Philistine oracle to Samaria.
Baal-zebub is the fly-averting idol thought to rid the land of insects; Beelzebub becomes a New Testament name for Satan; idol-oracles answer only darkly.
Gill weighs the lattice as a network window, a broken balcony rail, or a skylight trodden through.

debates.question: Why is the idol called 'Baal-zebub,' lord of flies?

sides.position: an averter of flies, a protective charm against swarms of insects
a fly-shaped god of divination (myiomancy), consulted for oracles

themes: a step between us and death

schools.summary: 'Now the angel had said'; the definite Angel meets the envoys at hill-set Samaria; 'king of Samaria' betrays Judaean feeling; 'no God in Israel' is the sting.
Gill takes the messenger as a common ministering spirit sent by the Lord, but hears the same rebuke of idolatry.
Wesley: to send to Baal-zebub is to treat the God of Israel as ignorant or powerless, as if the fly-god had more skill than the Maker of Israel.

debates.question: Who is 'the angel of the Lord' here?

sides.position: a common ministering angel sent by God
the covenant Angel, mediator of the invisible God's revelations

schools.summary: The sentence proves by the event that there is a God in Israel whose oracle is unerring; Ellicott suspects the compiler has abridged the account.
Gill fixes the contrast on the speaker — Jehovah, the only true God, over against an idol that is not.
Clarke: the word comes from the true God, who holds life and death; Baal-zebub can do nothing to reverse it.
The unrecognised prophet turns the embassy home; the king's emphatic 'Why have ye returned?' meets couriers who took the interposition for a divine warning.
Gill: from his habit, gravity, authority, and knowledge of their secret errand the messengers judged him a prophet and dared not go on.
The servants relay the oracle verbatim, shifting 'ye are going' to 'thou sendest' and lodging the guilt on the king; this is the second of three deliveries.
Gill supplies the plain report — 'there came a man up to meet us' — without comment or apology.
'What manner (mishpat) of man?' asks for external characteristics — figure, fashion, habit — the appearance that in a man of character mirrors the inner man.
Gill keeps it simple: the king asks after the man's fashion and clothing, half-suspecting the answer already.
'Lord of hair' means the rough hairy mantle, the badge of the prophetic office; the leather girdle of the poorest marks contempt for display and mourning for sin.
Clarke allows both a hairy garment and long hair and beard; Wesley leans to the mantle, worn also by John the Baptist to represent Elijah.
Gill allows either long unshorn hair and beard or a mantle of goat's or camel's hair like the Baptist's.

debates.question: Does 'a hairy man' mean his dress or his own hair?

sides.position: a rough hairy mantle, the mark of the prophetic office
his own long hair and beard (or either)

schools.summary: The army's fifties and their captains; hostile intent in the armed summons; 'the king hath said, Come down' sets royal power against the man of God, insulting the true God.
Clarke: it is impossible the king in such temper meant anything friendly; the fifty came to seize the prophet by force.
Matthew Henry: no alarms soften a secure sinner; the fire is called down not to save or avenge Elijah but to prove his mission — and the Lord forbade the disciples to imitate it (Luke 9:54).
Clarke: Elijah could no more make fire fall than the captain could; God alone, being just, sent it — the prophet's words are declarative, not imprecatory.
Ellicott: the phrase 'let fire come down from heaven' occurs only here and at the temple's dedication; the miracle is Jehovah vindicating his own cause, as on Carmel.
Gill: the captain called Elijah 'man of God' jeeringly, and now learns by the token that the title is true.

debates.question: Was Elijah's word a curse (imprecation) or a prophecy (declaration)?

sides.position: declarative — an announcement of what God had already determined
God's own act of self-vindication by fire, in the line of Carmel

schools.summary: Ellicott (with a Lapide): the second captain was more impudent, adding 'quickly' though he had heard the fate of the first.
Wesley: the captain shows more petulancy, unmoved by the former example.
Gill traces the insolence to the throne — the king, unterrified by the judgment, sends another.
Matthew Henry: a man resolved not to be baffled by Omnipotence must at last be conquered by the fire of hell, since fire from heaven will not subdue him.

Ellicott (with Thenius): the repeated destruction emphasises the prophet's authority and God's protection; the fifties are instruments of a will opposing Jehovah, so questions of comparative guilt fall away.
Gill records the plain fact: the same appeal, the same fire, the same end for the second captain and his fifty.
Ellicott weighs the verbs of submission — 'fell,' 'besought,' 'precious' — and the stress laid on the fifty lives now hanging on a word.
Gill: it showed the king dreadfully hardened to send a third fifty; the captain, unlike the others, climbed the hill and knelt.
Wesley: the kneeling expresses reverence and dread; nothing is got by contending with God — the wise learn submission from the ruin of the obstinate.
Gill: the captain owns the facts, ascribes the deaths to the true cause, and comes with an awful sense of God's judgment.
Ellicott's emphatic 'and now, this time' marks him off from the two who perished; on his plea the angel bids Elijah go down to the king.
Matthew Henry: God is as ready to show mercy to those who submit as he is severe to the defiant; none ever cast themselves on his mercy in vain.
'Go down; be not afraid of him' — of the captain, who as the king's tool might have slain the prophet; Elijah follows to the king and repeats the word.
Clarke: Elijah had neither will nor design of his own but obeyed as the ambassador of the King eternal, careless of his own safety.
Matthew Henry: he had refused the summonses not from fear but to avoid being imperiously compelled, and now goes boldly, magnifying his office.
The accusing question is parenthetic and the oracle is thrice repeated verbatim; Ellicott (with Baehr) reads Elijah's sudden coming and going as a mark of oral tradition.
Wesley: Elijah delivers the doom to the king's face, and the king dares not touch him, daunted by the prophet's confidence and power with God.
Gill: admitted to the chamber, Elijah confirms to the king's face exactly what he first sent by the messengers.

themes: the sentence to the king's face

schools.summary: Gill: Ahaziah died of the sickness on the bed, as the oracle said, and dying childless was succeeded by his brother Jehoram.
Clarke and Wesley harmonise the dates by co-regency — Jehoram of Judah made viceroy during the Ramoth-gilead war, so his 'second year' answers Jehoshaphat's eighteenth.
Ellicott suspects the text — LXX adds 'his brother' and reads 'eighteenth year,' probably original; either the Hebrew is corrupt or the compiler used another source.

debates.question: How is the date 'second year of Jehoram son of Jehoshaphat' to be reconciled with 2 Kings 3:1?

sides.position: by overlapping co-regencies (viceroyalty during the Ramoth-gilead war)
by text-criticism — the Greek 'eighteenth year' or a compiler's differing source

schools.summary: 'The acts' (dibre) points to the lost 'book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel,' a source the writer cites but which has not survived.
Gill: his acts must be few, for his two years' reign was itself imperfect and cut short — two years against his father's twenty-two.
Matthew Henry: some sinners God lets live long to aggravate judgment, others he cuts down soon to hasten it, but evil overtakes them all.
